target.name: (+)-selaginedorffone B
target.motivation: Emerging biological activity (cytotoxic against MCF-7 cells with IC50 of 9.0 µM) and intricate molecular architecture.

success_criteria.free_text: First enantioselective total synthesis, validation of biosynthesis, and comparison of NMR data with reported structure.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bonds forming the spiro[5.5]undecane motif
key_disconnections.reasoning: Bio-inspired approach, mimicking proposed biosynthesis from two modified abietane diterpenoids (diene-ol 3 and dienone 4).

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bonds forming the abietane skeleton
key_disconnections.reasoning: Inspired by Corey's work, to construct the highly functionalized trans-decalin system of the abietane scaffold.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy aims for a bio-inspired Diels-Alder reaction to construct the complex tetraterpenoid core, preceded by the enantioselective synthesis of the abietane monomers. Computational analysis was used to understand the regio- and diastereoselectivity of the Diels-Alder reaction.

thought.gap: Need to introduce an isopropenyl group and prepare for Claisen rearrangement.
thought.decision: To introduce the isopropenyl group, chose isopropenylmagnesium bromide addition, expecting allylic alcohol 9.

outcome.observation: Allylic alcohol 9 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Starting with the literature known compound 3-(3-isopropyl 4-methoxy)-propionaldehyde was converted to allylic alcohol 9 with isopropenylmagnesium bromide in 94% yield.

thought.gap: Need to rearrange the carbon skeleton and prepare for reduction.
thought.decision: To rearrange the carbon skeleton, chose Johnson–(orthoester)Claisen rearrangement, expecting ester 10.

outcome.observation: Ester 10 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: The Jhonson–(orthoester)Claisen rearrangement of 9 followed by DIBAL-H reduction furnished aldehyde 11 in 75% yield over two steps (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: Need to reduce the ester to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To reduce the ester, chose DIBAL-H reduction, expecting aldehyde 11.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde 11 was obtained. Overall yield for steps 2 and 3 is 75%.

evidence.text_span: The Jhonson–(orthoester)Claisen rearrangement of 9 followed by DIBAL-H reduction furnished aldehyde 11 in 75% yield over two steps (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: Need to introduce a TMS-acetylene group.
thought.decision: To introduce the TMS-acetylene group, chose TMS-acetylene addition, expecting alcohol (+-)-12.

outcome.observation: Propargyl alcohol (+-)-12 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Next, TMS-acetylene addition of 12 followed by DMP oxidation afforded ynone 13 in 81% yield over two steps (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: Need to oxidize the alcohol to a ketone.
thought.decision: To oxidize the alcohol, chose DMP oxidation, expecting ynone 13.

outcome.observation: Ynone 13 was obtained. Overall yield for steps 4 and 5 is 81%.

evidence.text_span: Next, TMS-acetylene addition of 12 followed by DMP oxidation afforded ynone 13 in 81% yield over two steps (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: Need to perform an enantioselective reduction of the ketone.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselective reduction, chose Noyori's reduction, expecting propargyl alcohol (+)-12 with high ee.

outcome.observation: Propargyl alcohol (+)-12 was obtained with high enantiomeric excess.

evidence.text_span: Gratifyingly, Noyori's reduction22 using a 1 mol% catalyst (see the ESI† for details) furnished propargyl alcohol (+)-12 in 92% yield with 99% ee (Fig. 3).

thought.gap: Need to remove the TMS protecting group.
thought.decision: To remove the TMS group, chose K2CO3 in methanol, expecting desilylated alcohol.

outcome.observation: Desilylated propargyl alcohol was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Later, desilylation of (+)-12 by treatment of K2CO3 in methanol followed by TBS-protection with tert-butyldimethylsilyl chloride afforded 14 in 90% over two steps.

thought.gap: Need to protect the alcohol as a TBS ether.
thought.decision: To protect the alcohol, chose TBSCl and imidazole, expecting TBS-protected propargyl alcohol 14.

outcome.observation: TBS-protected propargyl alcohol 14 was obtained. Overall yield for steps 7 and 8 is 90%.

evidence.text_span: Later, desilylation of (+)-12 by treatment of K2CO3 in methanol followed by TBS-protection with tert-butyldimethylsilyl chloride afforded 14 in 90% over two steps.

thought.gap: Need to form the abietane core via cyclization.
thought.decision: To form the abietane core, chose In(III)-catalyzed alkyne-activated ene-cyclization, expecting carbotricycle 15.

outcome.observation: Carbotricycle 15 with an exocyclic double bond was obtained.

evidence.text_span: At this stage In(III)-catalyzed alkyne-activated ene-cyclization of 14, 15 in the presence of 20 mol% InBr3 furnished carbotricycle having an exocyclic double bond, 15 in 74% yield (see the ESI** for details).

thought.gap: Need to deprotect the TBS ether to reveal the allylic alcohol.
thought.decision: To deprotect the TBS group, chose TBAF, expecting allylic alcohol 16.

outcome.observation: Allylic alcohol 16 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Next, Swern oxidation of allyl alcohol 16 furnished α,β-unsaturated ketone 18 in 76% yield (see the ESI† for details),24 which

thought.gap: Need to oxidize the allylic alcohol to an α,β-unsaturated ketone.
thought.decision: To oxidize the alcohol, chose Swern oxidation, expecting α,β-unsaturated ketone 18.

outcome.observation: α,β-unsaturated ketone 18 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Next, Swern oxidation of allyl alcohol 16 furnished α,β-unsaturated ketone 18 in 76% yield (see the ESI† for details),24 which

thought.gap: Need to convert the ketone to a 1,3-butadiene.
thought.decision: To form the 1,3-butadiene, chose Wittig olefination, expecting 1,3-butadiene 19.

outcome.observation: 1,3-butadiene 19 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: upon Wittig olefination furnished 1,3-butadiene 19 in 88% yield (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: Need to perform allylic oxidation to access compound 20.
thought.decision: Attempted allylic oxidation under various conditions, expecting compound 20.

outcome.observation: All attempts to perform allylic oxidation were unsuccessful.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, all attempts to perform the allylic oxidation to access compound 20 under various oxidation conditions were unsuccessful (see the ESI** for detailed information).

thought.gap: Need to perform α-oxygenation to obtain α-hydroxy enone.
thought.decision: Attempted direct α-oxygenation of α,β-unsaturated ketone 18 under various conditions, expecting α-hydroxy enone.

outcome.observation: Only furnished dienone 21 in 72% yield, without isolation of the required α-hydroxy enone.

evidence.text_span: Also, attempts for direct α-oxygenation of α,β-unsaturated ketone 18 under various conditions only furnished dienone 21 in 72% yield25 (Scheme 2), without the isolation of the required α-hydroxy enone (see the ESI** for details).

thought.background: Direct α-oxygenation of enone 18 failed to yield the desired α-hydroxy enone.
thought.gap: Need an alternative route to introduce oxygen at the α-position.
thought.decision: To introduce oxygen at the α-position, chose a modified Rubottom oxidation, expecting Cα-silyloxy enone derivative 22.

outcome.observation: Cα-silyloxy enone derivative 22 was obtained with high diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Next, enone 18 was converted to Cα-silyloxy enone derivative 22 in two steps (>20:1 dr) via a modified Rubottom oxidation26 (Scheme 3), where the additives found to play an important role (see the ESI** for details).27

thought.gap: Need to convert the enone to a 1,3-butadiene.
thought.decision: To form the 1,3-butadiene, chose Wittig olefination, expecting 'S-cis' 1,3-butadiene 23.

outcome.observation: 'S-cis' 1,3-butadiene 23 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Furthermore, Wittig olefination of the silyloxy enone 22 provided us with the ‘S-cis’ 1,3-butadiene 23 in 84% yield.28

thought.gap: Need to perform benzylic oxidation.
thought.decision: To perform benzylic oxidation, chose CrO3 in acetic acid, expecting compound 24.

outcome.observation: Compound 24 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: With the silyloxy butadiene compound, we performed benzylic oxidation with CrO3 in acetic acid to afford 24 (78% yield).29

thought.gap: Need to desilylate the TBS ether to obtain the diene-ol.
thought.decision: To desilylate, chose TBAF, expecting electron-rich diene-ol 25.

outcome.observation: Diene-ol 25 was obtained. X-ray structure showed formation of epimeric allylic alcohol of diene-ol 3.

evidence.text_span: Next, TBAF mediated desilylation of compound 24 afforded the electron-rich diene-ol partner 25 in 86% yield (Scheme 3). The X-ray structure of diene-ol 25 (CCDC 2259154) showed the formation of epimeric allylic alcohol of diene-ol 3 (Fig. 1).

thought.background: Diene-ol 25 is available, but it is the epimer of the desired diene-ol 3.
thought.gap: Need to invert the stereogenic center of the hydroxy group.
thought.decision: To invert the stereochemistry, chose Mitsunobu inversion with p-nitrobenzoic acid followed by saponification, expecting diene-ol 3.

outcome.observation: Diene-ol 3 (HOMO partner) was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Thus, a Mitsunobu inversion of the stereogenic center of the hydroxy group of 25 with p-nitrobenzoic acid30 followed by saponification afforded diene-ol 3 (HOMO partner) in 82% yield over two steps (Scheme 3).

thought.gap: Need to synthesize dienone 4 for the Diels-Alder reaction.
thought.decision: Attempted DMP oxidation of diene-ol 25, expecting dienone 4.

outcome.observation: Afforded heptacyclic enone 27 in 94% yield, without formation of any trace of dienone 4 or allyl alcohol 26. A clean spot-to-spot reaction on TLC was observed, indicating immediate reaction and further oxidation.

evidence.text_span: However, serendipitously, attempts to synthesize dienone 4 from a DMP (Dess–Martin periodinane) oxidation (1.1 equivalents) of the diene-ol 25 afforded the heptacyclic enone 27 in 94% yield, without the formation of any trace of neither enone 4 nor allyl alcohol 26 (a clean spot-to-spot reaction on TLC was observed; Scheme 3).

thought.gap: Need to synthesize dienone 4 for the Diels-Alder reaction.
thought.decision: Attempted Ley–Griffith oxidation of diene-ol 25, expecting dienone 4.

outcome.observation: Similar results were obtained, heptacyclic enone 27 was isolated in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, similar results were obtained under the well-known Ley–Griffith oxidation (TPAP) and heptacyclic enone 27 was isolated in 91% yield (see the ESI** for details).31

thought.gap: Need to synthesize dienone 4 for the Diels-Alder reaction, but previous attempts led to immediate formation of the heptacyclic product.
thought.decision: Attempted DMP oxidation of diene-ol 3, expecting dienone 4, but anticipating the same rapid reaction to heptacyclic enone 27.

outcome.observation: Exactly a similar result was obtained, heptacyclic enone 27 was formed in 97% yield. This confirmed the Curtin–Hammett conditions where dienone 4 reacts immediately with diene-ol 3, followed by a second oxidation to 27. X-ray analysis of 27 confirmed the stereogenic centers.

evidence.text_span: However, the biogenetic hypothesis of selaginedorffone B (2) suggests that the electron rich diene partner should contain the hydroxy group ‘syn’ with respect to the angular methyl group. To our pleasant surprise, exactly a similar result was obtained when DMP oxidation (1.1 equivalents) was carried out by taking the diene-ol 3 at 25 °C for 2 h (97% yield) (Scheme 3). From this serendipitous discovery, it can be considered that as soon as the formation of dienone 4 has taken place, it immediately reacted with diene-ol (either 25 or 3), by establishing the Curtin–Hammett conditions, and underwent the 2nd level of oxidation of the adduct (either 26 or 6) to form 27. Several control reactions were performed by changing the equivalents of the oxidizing agent to stop the reaction at the heptacyclic allyl alcohol stage (either 26 or 6). However, we were not successful in this regard. By lowering the oxidizing agent to 0.5 equivalent, we could see the formation of only 27 (~43–46% yields) in addition to the starting diene-ol 25 or 3 (~44–48% yields), respectively (see the ESI** for detailed studies). At this stage what was left was to perform a chemoselective reduction of α,β-unsaturated enone of 27 over three keto functional groups. However, attempts towards this direction via reduction under Luche conditions led to the formation of an inseparable mixture of alcohol/allylic alcohols.

thought.background: Heptacyclic enone 27 is available, but it has three keto functional groups.
thought.gap: Need to perform a chemoselective reduction of the α,β-unsaturated enone over the other ketones.
thought.decision: Attempted Luche reduction, expecting selective reduction of the enone.

outcome.observation: Led to the formation of an inseparable mixture of alcohol/allylic alcohols, indicating non-selective reduction.

evidence.text_span: However, attempts towards this direction via reduction under Luche conditions led to the formation of an inseparable mixture of alcohol/allylic alcohols.

thought.background: Heptacyclic enone 27 is available, with multiple ketones.
thought.gap: Need to chemoselectively protect the ketones over the enone.
thought.decision: Attempted chemoselective protection of ketones.

outcome.observation: Chemoselective protection of ketones over the enone 27 proved to be difficult.

evidence.text_span: Also, chemoselective protection of ketones over the enone 27 proved to be difficult; therefore, an alternate route was inevitable at this point.

thought.background: Previous attempts to form dienone 4 and subsequent selective reduction of 27 failed.
thought.gap: Need an alternate route to perform the Diels-Alder reaction that avoids the benzylic ketone.
thought.decision: To avoid the benzylic ketone, chose to perform the Diels-Alder reaction with diene-ol 20, which was synthesized via desilylation of TBS-ether 23.

outcome.observation: Diene-ol 20 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Thus, it was thought of performing the Diels–Alder reaction with diene-ol 20 that avoids benzylic ketone, which was synthesized via the desilylation of TBS-ether 23 (Scheme 4).

thought.gap: Need to perform the Diels-Alder reaction and subsequent oxidation to form the heptacyclic enone.
thought.decision: To form the heptacyclic enone, chose DMP oxidation of diene-ol 20 under standard conditions, expecting enone 30 via non-isolable intermediates dienone 28 and allyl alcohol 29.

outcome.observation: Heptacyclic enone 30 was obtained. A clean spot-to-spot reaction on TLC was observed, indicating rapid formation of 30. X-ray analysis of 30 confirmed the required stereogenic centers.

evidence.text_span: As per our optimized conditions, the DMP oxidation (1.1 equivalents) was carried out by taking the diene-ol 20 under standard conditions (25 °C for 2 h) to furnish 96% yield of enone 30 via the non-isolable intermediates dienone 28 and allyl alcohol 29 (a clean spot-to-spot reaction on TLC was observed; Scheme 4). Furthermore, X-ray analysis of heptacyclic enone 30 (CCDC 2283581) unambiguously confirmed the required stereogenic centers present in the heptacyclic skeleton of (+)-2 (Scheme 4).

thought.background: Heptacyclic enone 30 is available, with multiple ketones.
thought.gap: Need to differentiate the enone over the other ketones for selective reduction.
thought.decision: Attempted Luche reduction in MeOH, expecting selective reduction of the enone.

outcome.observation: Led to a mixture of inseparable diastereomeric alcohols (2:1) forming from the reduction of ketone, keeping the enone intact.

evidence.text_span: Next, an attempt to differentiate the enone over the ketone in 30 under the Luche reduction in MeOH was conducted. To our surprise, this reaction led to a mixture of inseparable diastereomeric alcohols (2:1) forming from the reduction of ketone, keeping the enone intact (see the ESI† for details).

thought.background: Selective reduction of enone 30 failed due to non-selective reduction of other ketones.
thought.gap: Need to protect the other ketones before reducing the enone.
thought.decision: To protect the ketones, chose ketal protection using ethylene glycol and Bi(OTf)3, expecting compound 31.

outcome.observation: Ketal-protected compound 31 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: After an exhaustive optimization ketal protection of ketone 30, in the presence of Bi(OTf)3, refluxing toluene afforded compound 31 in 83% yield.32

thought.gap: Need to selectively reduce the enone to an allylic alcohol.
thought.decision: To selectively reduce the enone, chose Luche reduction, expecting allylic alcohol 32.

outcome.observation: Allylic alcohol 32 was obtained with high diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Next, Luche reduction of the enone 31 at 0 °C provided allyl alcohol 32 in 94% yield with >20:1 dr (Scheme 4).

thought.gap: Need to protect the alcohol and then perform benzylic oxidation.
thought.decision: To protect the alcohol, chose acetylation, followed by benzylic oxidation, expecting diketone 33.

outcome.observation: Diketone 33 was obtained over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Furthermore, acetylation of allyl alcohol 32 followed by benzylic oxidation afforded diketone 33 in 70% yield over two steps.

thought.gap: Need to deprotect the ketal and saponify the acetate.
thought.decision: To deprotect and saponify, chose 4(N) HCl followed by K2CO3, expecting methyl ether of (+)-selaginedorffone B (compound 6).

outcome.observation: Methyl ether of (+)-selaginedorffone B (compound 6) was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Later, ketal deprotection using 4(N) HCl followed by the saponification of acetate cleanly furnished methyl ether of (+)-2 i.e. compound 6 in 84% yield over two steps (Scheme 4).

thought.background: Methyl ether of (+)-selaginedorffone B (compound 6) is available.
thought.gap: Need to demethylate the aryl methyl ether to obtain the final product.
thought.decision: To demethylate, chose sodium ethanethiolate at elevated temperature, expecting (+)-selaginedorffone B [(+)-2].

outcome.observation: The reported structure of (+)-selaginedorffone B [(+)-2] was obtained. However, NMR spectra exhibited notable discrepancies compared to those reported by Long et al.

evidence.text_span: Finally, demethylation with sodium ethanethiolate33 of the aryl methyl ether 6 at elevated temperature afforded the reported structure of the tetraterpenoid, (+)-selaginedorffone B [(+)-2] (Scheme 4), in 94% yield. Unfortunately, NMR spectra of the synthetic one, (+)-2, exhibited notable discrepancies compared to those reported by Long et al.4

thought.background: Compound 6 is available. NMR discrepancies with the reported structure of (+)-selaginedorffone B were observed.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize the epimer of the hydroxy group of the final allyl alcohol to compare its spectral data.
thought.decision: To synthesize the epimer, chose a sequential Mitsunobu reaction followed by K2CO3-mediated hydrolysis, expecting epimeric allyl alcohol 34.

outcome.observation: Epimeric allyl alcohol 34 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, compound 6 was charged for a sequential Mitsunobu reaction30 followed by K2CO3-mediated hydrolysis of the benzoate derivative, yielding the epimeric allyl alcohol 34, with a 72% yield over two steps.

thought.gap: Need to demethylate the aryl methyl ether to obtain the C2-epimer of the reported structure.
thought.decision: To demethylate, chose sodium ethanethiolate, expecting the C2-epimer of (+)-selaginedorffone B.

outcome.observation: The C2-epimer of the reported structure of selaginedorffone B was synthesized. Regrettably, discrepancies were once again encountered in this process.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, like the previous procedure, the aryl methyl ether was demethylated with sodium ethanethiolate to synthesize the C2-epimer of the reported structure of selaginedorffone B (+)-2 (Scheme 4).33 Regrettably, discrepancies were once again encountered in this process.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated for the entire synthesis, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The total synthesis was accomplished in 23 longest linear steps.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total steps not explicitly stated, but 23 LLS is given.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Five distinct failed attempts were reported during the synthesis.

validation.comparison_to_prior: NMR spectra of the synthetic (+)-selaginedorffone B and its C2-epimer exhibited notable discrepancies compared to those reported by Long et al. However, X-ray crystallographic studies of late-stage intermediates (27 and 30) and comprehensive 2D NMR analysis strongly suggest that the structure of the synthetic sample unequivocally matches the reported structure by Long et al.
validation.significance: First catalytic enantioselective gram-scale total synthesis of the reported structure of (+)-selaginedorffone B. The synthesis validates the biosynthesis of (+)-selaginedorffone B through a unique Diels-Alder reaction under ambient conditions. Computational studies unveil complex molecular interactions driving the Diels-Alder reaction.

source.title: Biomimetic total synthesis of the reported structure of (+)-selaginedorffone B.
source.authors: Kundu S, Jana D, Mandal N, Mondal A, Murmu R, Roy NK, Datta A, Bisai A.
